title: Environment bootstrap templates (detailed)
description: Detailed instructions for an automated assistant (Copilot) to create reproducible local development environments and container helpers. This file is a guide: Copilot MUST adapt the templates to the target repository rather than copying verbatim.
usage_short: Give this JSON to Copilot in another repo. Copilot should detect repo files (requirements, devcontainer) and either create or adapt the helper files. The JSON contains templates with placeholders and rules to follow.
rules_for_copilot: Do not blindly copy files into the target repo. Inspect the repository first: look for requirements.txt, requirements-dev.txt, requirements_phase2*.txt, .devcontainer, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml.
If requirements files exist, use them. If they don't exist, create a minimal requirements.txt containing 'requests' and document what the user must add.

Replace placeholder '{{VENV_NAME}}' with the configured venv name (default: .venv-Z1.1). If the target repo already has a different venv convention, prefer that convention and document the difference.
If the target OS is non-Windows (detectable via repo or a user-specified flag), produce shell scripts (bash) instead of PowerShell.
When adding files, add a short header comment describing why the file was created and include a 'DO NOT COPY VERBATIM' note so future assistants know to adapt it.
If the repo already contains a .vscode/settings.json, merge settings instead of overwriting; add only the python.defaultInterpreterPath and python.terminal.activateEnvironment keys if missing.
Ask the user for missing pieces (preferred python version, private package indexes, API key handling) if they cannot be inferred safely.
Always keep secrets out of version control; if a .env is required, create .env.example and add .env to .gitignore if it's not already ignored.
copilot_prompt: You are given this JSON guide. Adapt it to the current repository: detect existing requirement files and devcontainer config, choose the correct venv name (default .venv-Z1.1), and create helper scripts or docs. Confirm any guesses with the user via a summary comment at the top of the generated files.

templates.purpose: PowerShell helper to create and bootstrap a local Python virtual environment and install requirements.
templates.content_template:
<#
Create and bootstrap the local virtual environment '{{VENV_NAME}}' for Windows PowerShell.
DO NOT COPY VERBATIM: This template must be adapted to the repo (requirements filenames, python version).
Usage: .\scripts\create_env_{{VENV_NAME}}.ps1 [-InstallPhase2]
#>
param( [switch]$InstallPhase2 )
Write-Host "Creating environment {{VENV_NAME}} (PowerShell)" -ForegroundColor Cyan
# Ensure python is available
try { $pyCmd = Get-Command python -ErrorAction Stop } catch { Write-Host "Python not found. Install Python first." -ForegroundColor Red; exit 1 }
Write-Host "Python detected: $($pyCmd.Path)" -ForegroundColor Green
if (-not (Test-Path ".\{{VENV_NAME}}")) { Write-Host "Creating virtual environment {{VENV_NAME}}..."; python -m venv {{VENV_NAME}} } else { Write-Host "Virtual environment already exists: {{VENV_NAME}}" }
if (-not (Test-Path ".\{{VENV_NAME}}\Scripts\Activate.ps1")) { Write-Host "Activation script not found; aborting." -ForegroundColor Red; exit 1 }
Write-Host "Activating {{VENV_NAME}} in current shell..."; . .\{{VENV_NAME}}\Scripts\Activate.ps1
Write-Host "Upgrading pip and installing requirements..."
# Install recommended files if present
if (Test-Path "requirements.txt") { python -m pip install -r requirements.txt }
if (Test-Path "requirements-dev.txt") { python -m pip install -r requirements-dev.txt }
if ($InstallPhase2) { if (Test-Path "requirements_phase2.txt") { python -m pip install -r requirements_phase2.txt } }
Write-Host "
Done. Verify with: python -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'" -ForegroundColor Green

templates.purpose: Bash helper to create and bootstrap a local Python virtual environment and install requirements on POSIX systems.
templates.content_template:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Create and bootstrap a venv named {{VENV_NAME}}
set -euo pipefail
VENV={{VENV_NAME}}
if [ ! -d "$VENV" ]; then python -m venv "$VENV"; fi
# shellcheck source=/dev/null
source "$VENV/bin/activate"
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
[ -f requirements.txt ] && python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
[ -f requirements-dev.txt ] && python -m pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
if [ "$1" = "--phase2" ]; then [ -f requirements_phase2.txt ] && python -m pip install -r requirements_phase2.txt; fi
printf "\nDone. Verify with: python -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'\n"

templates.purpose: Helper that builds docker-compose and optionally starts containers.
templates.content_template:
<# Build and run docker-compose for local dev. This template checks for docker-compose and runs build/up. DO NOT COPY VERBATIM - adapt paths if repo places docker-compose elsewhere. #>
param([switch]$BuildOnly)
if (-not (Get-Command docker-compose -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { Write-Host "docker-compose not found in PATH; please install Docker Desktop." -ForegroundColor Red; exit 1 }
Write-Host "[devcontainer] Building docker-compose images..."
docker-compose build --no-cache
if ($BuildOnly) { Write-Host "Build complete; skipping start."; exit 0 }
Write-Host "[devcontainer] Starting containers (attached)..."
docker-compose up --remove-orphans

templates.purpose: Human-readable guide explaining both local venv and devcontainer workflows and verification steps.
templates.content_template:
# Devcontainer & Local Environment Quick Start

This document explains two reproducible workflows for development: a local Python virtualenv and a Docker-based VS Code devcontainer.

1) Local venv (fast)

- Run the repo's create script (PowerShell or Bash) to create `{{VENV_NAME}}` and install requirements.
- Example: `./scripts/create_env_{{VENV_NAME}}.ps1` (Windows) or `./scripts/create_env_{{VENV_NAME}}.sh` (POSIX).
- Verify: `python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"` returns the venv path.

2) Devcontainer (exact environment)

- Open the repo in VS Code and run: Command Palette → Dev Containers: Reopen in Container.
- The container builds system-level packages and installs pip packages listed in requirements files.
- Alternative: run the helper script: `./scripts/devcontainer_build.ps1` (PowerShell) or use docker-compose directly.

Notes:
- These helpers install packages listed in `requirements.txt` and `requirements-dev.txt`. They do not install packages not listed — keep requirements pinned for reproducibility.
- Do not commit secrets. Use `.env.example` and copy to `.env` locally.

verify_commands:
# After creating the venv (PowerShell)
python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"

# Check installed packages and run lint/tests
python -m pip list
python -m pip check
ruff check .
python -m pytest -q
examples_and_notes: This JSON intentionally contains placeholders ({{VENV_NAME}}). When Copilot creates files, it should replace placeholders based on repository conventions or ask the user. The assistant must not copy the templates verbatim into other repos without adapting them.
